nodes.label: ADR-0001: Atlas is the source of truth
nodes.norm_label: adr-0001: atlas is the source of truth

nodes.label: Atlas is the source of truth (decision)
nodes.rationale: Adding a second persistence/execution stack (Postgres/Drizzle, Supabase Auth, PocketBase, a second executor) would create two sources of truth and make failure/recovery semantics ambiguous. Atlas already owns auth, RBAC, persistence, workers, jobs, workflows, execution, triggers, artifacts, deliveries, audit, usage, and SSE. Keeping one source of truth means fewer services to deploy/back up and no frontend schema migration when Atlas storage evolves; the cost is that frontend capability is bounded by the Atlas API.
nodes.norm_label: atlas is the source of truth (decision)

nodes.label: thClaws workers
nodes.rationale: Worker credentials, routing policy, audit, and artifact safety stay behind Atlas; browser code never calls thClaws directly.

nodes.label: Typed transport / BFF layer
nodes.rationale: The transport layer is allowed only for secure cookies, SSR, CORS isolation, bearer forwarding, response normalization, and SSE proxying. It must not become a new domain backend with persistence or execution logic.

nodes.label: Authentication boundary
nodes.rationale: Atlas authenticates with opaque per-user bearer tokens only and issues no cookie, so flow-designer mints/owns the browser session cookie (sealed httpOnly) holding the Atlas bearer server-side. Each private server function validates the session and calls a typed fixed Atlas op because the RPC endpoint is directly reachable and a route beforeLoad is only a UI navigation boundary.

nodes.label: Atlas opaque bearer token
nodes.rationale: Kept server-side in an httpOnly cookie; must never reach browser code, localStorage, or a URL query string (including Atlas's ?token= EventSource path) because proxies, logs, and history could capture it.

nodes.label: Same-origin authenticated SSE transport
nodes.rationale: Rather than use Atlas's ?token= query-param EventSource path (which would expose the bearer to the browser), a flow-designer server route reads the session cookie and forwards to Atlas with the Authorization header server-side, relaying the stream to the browser.

nodes.label: State layers (Atlas/Query/local/Zustand)
nodes.rationale: Atlas holds durable/execution state; TanStack Query holds the server cache and invalidation; React/local holds ephemeral UI state; Zustand is UI-only and must never hold workers/jobs/workflows/runs.

nodes.label: Job event SSE contract
nodes.rationale: GET /api/jobs/{job_id}/events?after=<seq> streams text/event-stream; after is an exclusive lower bound (the only resume param, no Last-Event-ID); dedupe by id/seq; normal end is an explicit event: close with terminal state; EOF without close is a disconnect to reconnect with backoff; there is no heartbeat so idle streams are exposed to proxy timeouts.

nodes.label: Workflow node compatibility matrix
nodes.rationale: Of the UI's eight NodeKinds only four map to native graph nodes; condition/loop/fanout are edge/graph constructs and trigger is a separate resource. Unknown Atlas node/condition types must fail closed, never be sent to Atlas.

nodes.label: Four native node types (worker/manager/join/human_gate)
nodes.rationale: Atlas's executor accepts exactly four node type strings (workflows.py:173). The canvas palette exposes only these; 'approval' is the display label for internal kind human_gate, not an API alias, migrated once from the mock scaffold in Phase 3.

nodes.label: Role and permission matrix (UI gating only)
nodes.rationale: Roles are exactly admin/operator/viewer/auditor, enforced centrally in Atlas _dispatch. The frontend mirrors the table only to hide/disable actions (UX); it is never the security boundary because a role can change server-side.
nodes.norm_label: role and permission matrix (ui gating only)

nodes.label: Workflow definition graph adapter
nodes.rationale: The canvas must serialize the Atlas semantic graph, not the React Flow layout. Always emit every edge condition, join.mode/quorum, and manager.schema; strip layout and unknown UI fields. Round-trip fixtures for the four native kinds are required before wiring save/run.

nodes.label: Atlas is the only authorization authority
nodes.rationale: Do not build a second RBAC system in the frontend. Frontend role/permission data is UX-only (hide/disable). A cookie-cached role is never a security decision because a role can change in Atlas at any time and Atlas enforces permissions on every call.
nodes.norm_label: atlas is the only authorization authority

nodes.label: *.functions.ts createServerFn RPC boundary
nodes.rationale: Routes/components/loaders/hooks may statically import *.functions.ts; the bundler replaces the body with a network call so server code never ships to the browser. Do not dynamically import them or the transform will not apply.

nodes.label: *.server.ts server-only modules
nodes.rationale: Holds Atlas HTTP clients, session/cookie helpers, credentials, and secrets. Client code must never import them; process.env reads happen only in request-time server execution.

nodes.label: Client/server import boundary
nodes.rationale: Client may import types, secret-free mappers, query hooks, and *.functions.ts; it may not import *.server.ts. Enforced by an ESLint no-restricted-imports rule verified with a probe import.

nodes.label: createCsrfMiddleware requirement
nodes.rationale: Because this repo defines src/start.ts, TanStack Start does not auto-install CSRF protection; createCsrfMiddleware() must be installed in the start requestMiddleware before any auth or mutation server function. The origin matcher is only consulted when Sec-Fetch-Site is absent (reverse-proxy case).

nodes.label: Keep-alive connection desync after rejected POST
nodes.rationale: Atlas advertises HTTP/1.1 keep-alive but rejects 401/403 requests before reading the request body; the undrained body poisons the next pooled request, which BaseHTTPRequestHandler answers as 501 HTML. Found in Phase 1 against Atlas 595ef62. Mitigated in atlas-api.server.ts by sending Connection: close on every POST and validating content-type before JSON parse; regression-tested. Do not remove until Atlas drains/refuses the body on rejection paths.
nodes.norm_label: keep-alive connection desync after rejected post

nodes.label: Atlas auth token lifecycle (non-expiring tokens)
nodes.rationale: Production release blocker: api_tokens has no expires_at, every login mints a new orphaned-but-valid 'dashboard login' token, and there is no login rate limiting. A flow-designer session cookie expiring does not revoke the underlying Atlas token. Mitigated only by calling POST /api/auth/logout and bounding the session cookie to 8 hours.

nodes.label: Single-node SQLite persistence and runtime
nodes.rationale: Atlas uses SQLite with an in-process lock and in-process background threads for workflow/job execution: one active primary only, no active-active cluster, no read-replica/failover for live run state. The frontend cannot and must not try to make Atlas horizontally scalable.

nodes.label: Asymmetric event streaming (job SSE vs run poll)
nodes.rationale: Only per-job events are SSE (with after cursor, terminal close, no heartbeat); workflow-run events are poll-only persisted JSON (limit-only, no cursor). There is no unified live stream for a whole workflow graph, so combine per-job SSE keyed on each runtime node's job_id with run refetch.
nodes.norm_label: asymmetric event streaming (job sse vs run poll)

nodes.label: No workflow layout persistence endpoint
nodes.rationale: Atlas stores only the semantic graph; there is no endpoint to persist node positions/viewport. Store layout locally in localStorage keyed by workflow id + graph version with an auto-layout fallback; it does not sync across devices/users.

nodes.label: httpOnly session cookie / useSession strategy
nodes.rationale: Use TanStack Start's useSession (sealed cookie via SESSION_SECRET, single key, rotation forces re-login) rather than hand-rolled crypto. sessionHeader:false is mandatory so the httpOnly cookie is the only accepted carrier. Cookie Secure is driven by the PUBLIC_ORIGIN scheme, not NODE_ENV (which silently defaults to development), to avoid failing open on HTTPS deployments. Session max age is 8 hours.

nodes.label: Vitest (adapter/unit/contract/stream)
nodes.rationale: Chosen because it is already a Vite project, so Vitest reuses the existing config and TS paths with near-zero setup. Mock-only tests are not sufficient for the release gate; contract tests must run against a real/fixture Atlas.

nodes.label: Playwright (browser/e2e)
nodes.rationale: Drives real login, SSE reconnect, canvas, and two-tab flows against a running frontend plus local Atlas.

nodes.label: Phased delivery plan (Phase 0-7)
nodes.rationale: Sequential phases (Contract, Transport/auth, Read-only, Mutations/editor, Live events, Operational pages, Security/resilience, Verification/release), each ending with an explicit user-confirmation gate; a later phase does not start until the prior gate is confirmed.

nodes.label: Lovable published-history policy
nodes.rationale: The project is connected to Lovable; force-pushing or rebasing/amending/squashing already-pushed commits rewrites history on Lovable's side and the user could lose project history. Commits to the connected branch sync back into the Lovable editor, so keep the branch in a working state.

nodes.label: TanStack Start file-based routing
nodes.rationale: Every .tsx in src/routes defines a route; do not create Next.js/Remix conventions (src/pages, app/layout.tsx). The only root layout is __root.tsx and routeTree.gen.ts is auto-generated (never hand-edited).

nodes.label: Design tokens (src/styles.css)
nodes.rationale: Use design tokens from src/styles.css; do not introduce hardcoded colors (bg-black, text-white, bg-[#...]). Live logs must be bounded and virtualized, never rendered as an unbounded event list.
